instruction: I like controlling the game and winning slowly. What Legacy deck should I consider?
output: Great question! There are several strong control options in Legacy right now. Beanstalk Control is the most popular at 2.3% meta share - it uses Up the Beanstalk to generate card advantage off pitch spells like Force of Will and Solitude, then grinds opponents out with removal and countermagic. Jeskai Control is another option with Lightning Bolt and Swords to Plowshares for removal plus planeswalkers for inevitability. If you want something truly unique, Lands controls the game entirely through land abilities - Wasteland recursion, Maze of Ith, and Punishing Fire. What appeals to you more: traditional countermagic control, or something more unconventional?

instruction: I want to play combo but I do not want to lose to a single Force of Will. What are my options?
output: Smart thinking. The combo decks most resilient to Force of Will are: 1) The EPIC Storm (TES) - Burning Wish gives you multiple combo routes, and you can bait Force with a less important spell then go off. 2) Doomsday - you construct your pile to play around Force by including your own countermagic in the 5 cards. 3) Painter - you have Goblin Welder to recur combo pieces that get countered, plus Red Elemental Blast becomes an unconditional counter with Painter naming blue. 4) Aluren - the combo is repeatable, so a single Force only delays you one turn. What is your experience level with combo? Some of these require more skill than others.

instruction: I just started playing Legacy and have a small budget. What should I build?
output: Welcome to Legacy! Here are the best budget entry points: Oops All Spells (~$1100) is a tier 1 combo deck with zero dual lands. It can win on turn 1 and teaches you about Legacy's interaction patterns (Force of Will, graveyard hate). Burn (~$1500) is straightforward and teaches you about Legacy's speed and life total management. Red Stompy (~$2500) teaches you about prison strategies and lock pieces. All three are tournament-competitive. Which play style appeals to you: fast combo, aggressive burn, or disruptive prison?

instruction: I used to play Modern Izzet Murktide. What is the closest Legacy deck?
output: You will feel right at home with Izzet Delver in Legacy! It plays Murktide Regent, Dragon's Rage Channeler (in some builds), Lightning Bolt, and cantrips - all familiar from Modern. The key Legacy additions are Force of Will (free counterspell), Daze (free counter by returning an Island), Wasteland (land destruction), and Brainstorm (the best cantrip ever printed). The deck is currently tier 2 at 4.3% meta share. Dimir Tempo (UB instead of UR) is the tier 1 version that trades Bolt for Thoughtseize and Bowmasters. Would you like to start with UR for familiarity, or jump to UB for power?

instruction: I want to play a deck that nobody expects. Something that wins tournaments but is under 2% of the meta.
output: Love the rogue deck mindset! Here are some sleeper picks: Aluren (1.2%) just won the Legacy Showcase Qualifier - it is a combo-control hybrid where opponents rarely know how to sideboard. Cradle Control (2.3%) uses Gaea's Cradle for absurd mana generation with a Green Sun's Zenith toolbox. Stiflenought (0.1%) puts a 12/12 trample on the board turn 1-2 and protects it with Force. Esper Vial (0.2%) showed up in recent Challenge top 8s. The advantage of rogue decks is that opponents make more mistakes because they do not know your deck's key cards. What colors or play style interest you?

instruction: I want to build Dimir Tempo. What do I need?
output:
Here is the core shell for Dimir Tempo:

Threats (12): 4 Orcish Bowmasters, 4 Murktide Regent, 4 Tamiyo Inquisitive Student (or Barrowgoyf)

Cantrips (8): 4 Brainstorm, 4 Ponder

Disruption (12): 4 Force of Will, 4 Daze, 4 Thoughtseize

Removal (4): 2 Fatal Push, 2 Snuff Out

Lands (19): 4 Polluted Delta, 4 Misty Rainforest, 3 Underground Sea, 4 Wasteland, 2 Island, 1 Swamp, 1 Undercity Sewers

Sideboard: 4 Leyline of the Void, 2 Surgical Extraction, 2 Hydroblast, 2 Flusterstorm, 2 Null Rod, 2 Toxic Deluge, 1 Massacre

Total cost: ~$5000 with real duals, ~$2000 with Watery Grave substitutions. Would you like me to go through the card choices or discuss budget options?

instruction: Build me a deck that beats Dimir Tempo consistently.
output:
The best Dimir Tempo killers right now:

1) Lands (strong favorable): Wasteland-proof mana base, Maze of Ith blanks Murktide and Bowmasters, Punishing Fire grinds out small threats, and Dark Depths threatens a 20/20 they cannot remove. Main drawback: $8000+ cost and weak to fast combo.

2) Sneak and Show (favorable): Emrakul dodges all their removal and annihilator devastates their board. They need Force + Daze to stop you, and you have your own Force backup. Cost: $4000-5000.

3) Ocelot Pride Midrange (close but favorable post-board): Goes wide past their one-for-one removal, does not rely on cantrips so Bowmasters is less impactful. Cost: $2500.

Which of these interests you? I can build a specific list.

instruction: I have 4 Force of Will, 4 Brainstorm, 4 Ponder, and some fetchlands. What Legacy decks can I build with this core?
output:
That is an excellent starting point! Force of Will + cantrips is the foundation of every blue Legacy deck. Here is what you can build, roughly ordered by additional investment needed:

1) Dimir Tempo: Add Bowmasters, Murktide, Thoughtseize, Daze, Underground Sea. (~$1500-3000 additional)
2) Izzet Delver: Add Murktide, Lightning Bolt, Daze, Volcanic Island. (~$1500-2500 additional)
3) Sneak and Show: Add Show and Tell, Sneak Attack, Emrakul, Atraxa, Ancient Tomb, Lotus Petal. (~$1500-2000 additional)
4) Doomsday: Add Doomsday, Dark Ritual, Thassa's Oracle, Daze, Underground Sea. (~$1000-2000 additional)
5) Stiflenought: Add Stifle, Phyrexian Dreadnought, Daze, Dress Down. (~$500-1000 additional)

What is your budget for additional cards, and what play style do you prefer?

instruction: I pulled an Ocelot Pride and Guide of Souls from MH3. Can I build a deck around them?
output:
Absolutely! You have the core of the second most popular deck in Legacy - Ocelot Pride Midrange at 8.5% meta share. Here is the build:

Energy Package: 4 Ocelot Pride, 4 Guide of Souls, 4 Ajani Nacatl Pariah, 4 Amped Raptor

Removal: 4 Swords to Plowshares, 4 Solitude

Support: 4 Phelia Exuberant Shepherd, 2-4 Broadside Bombardiers

Lands: Arid Mesa, Marsh Flats, Windswept Heath, Plains, Mountain, Plateau (or Sacred Foundry)

Sideboard: Containment Priest, Mindbreak Trap, Deafening Silence, Meltdown, Surgical Extraction

This deck is relatively budget-friendly at $2500 with real Plateau, or $2200 with Sacred Foundry. The energy cards themselves are $10-30 each. It does not need Force of Will or Brainstorm, making it one of the cheapest tier 1 options. Want me to optimize the list for your local meta?

instruction: What if I cut the Dazes from my Dimir Tempo list for more removal?
output:
That is a common consideration but usually wrong for Dimir Tempo. Here is why Daze is so important:

1) Free interaction on the play: Turn 1 Delver, opponent plays a spell, Daze it - you are up a threat and a counter for zero mana.
2) Paired with Wasteland: Wasteland their land, then Daze their spell - they cannot pay the 1 because you destroyed their mana source.
3) Tempo defines the archetype: Without Daze, you are a midrange deck pretending to be tempo. Midrange is worse at the Dimir shell than pure tempo.
4) Combo protection: Daze protects your threats in the first 2-3 turns when it matters most.

That said, Daze IS correct to cut post-sideboard in grindy matchups where games go long and opponents have plenty of mana. Board out 1-2 Dazes against midrange and control, keep all 4 against combo. What matchup were you concerned about?

instruction: I keep losing to Chalice of the Void on 1. What should I change?
output:
Chalice on 1 is brutal against cantrip-heavy decks. Here are your options:

In-deck solutions: Force of Vigor (destroys Chalice for free by pitching a green card), Engineered Explosives on 0 (destroys Chalice when it has 0 charge counters, costs 0 so Chalice does not counter it - wait, Chalice on 1 does not stop EE on 0), Abrupt Decay (cannot be countered, destroys CMC 3 or less).

Deck choice solutions: Switch to a deck where 1-mana spells are not critical. Sneak and Show (combo costs 3-4), Lands (operates through land abilities), Ocelot Pride (creature-based, most threats cost 2+).

Sideboard plan: Bring in all your Chalice answers and cut your worst 1-mana spells. Typical: +2 Force of Vigor, +1 Engineered Explosives, -2 Ponder, -1 Thoughtseize.

Which deck are you playing? I can give specific advice.

instruction: My Izzet Delver list keeps losing to Orcish Bowmasters. How do I adjust?
output:
Bowmasters punishes your cantrips and kills your small creatures. Here are adjustments:

1) Threat selection: Play Murktide Regent (survives Bowmasters trigger and blocks the 1/1), cut Dragon's Rage Channeler (dies to the ping). Consider Ethereal Forager or Tolarian Terror as additional Bolt-proof threats.

2) Cantrip timing: Fetch and Brainstorm on your own turn only when necessary. Avoid end-of-turn Brainstorm against open BB mana - they will flash in Bowmasters in response.

3) Removal: Ensure you have ways to kill Bowmasters: Lightning Bolt, Unholy Heat (if running delirium), or Pyroblast if they are the UB version.

4) Consider switching to Dimir: If Bowmasters is everywhere in your meta, playing your own Bowmasters is better than trying to beat them. The UB shell handles the mirror better.

What does your current list look like?

instruction: I want to add Uro, Titan of Nature's Wrath to my control deck. Is it worth the green splash?
output:
Uro is an excellent grindy card but the green splash has real costs:

Pros: Uro gains 3 life (matters vs aggro), draws a card, ramps, and escapes as a 6/6. In long games, Uro is nearly unbeatable card advantage.

Cons: Adding green requires Tropical Island ($400-600) and green fetches, stretching the mana base. More nonbasics means more vulnerability to Wasteland and Blood Moon. You also need enough green cards for the splash to be worthwhile (Endurance, Veil of Summer in sideboard).

Verdict: If your meta is heavy on fair midrange and control, Uro is excellent. If your meta is combo-heavy, the splash weakens your mana base for a card you may never have time to cast. What does your local meta look like?

instruction: Should I play Ponder or Preordain as my second cantrip?
output:
Ponder in almost every case. Ponder looks at 3 cards and can shuffle them away with a fetchland if all 3 are bad, effectively seeing 4 cards. Preordain scrys 2 then draws 1, seeing only 3 cards with no shuffle option.

Ponder is better because: 1) It finds specific cards faster (important for combo and sideboard hate). 2) The shuffle option with fetchlands is extremely powerful. 3) It sets up Delver of Secrets flips by putting an instant/sorcery on top.

Preordain is better only when: 1) You have very few fetchlands (no shuffle synergy). 2) Your deck does not play Delver. 3) You need the scry to filter away truly dead cards.

In Legacy, the answer is Ponder 99% of the time since every blue deck runs fetchlands.

instruction: The Legacy metagame just shifted after a ban. How do I adapt my deck?
output:
When bans happen, follow this process:

1) Identify the power vacuum: What decks were kept in check by the banned card? Those decks will rise. When Entomb was banned, Reanimator dropped and Oops All Spells filled the fast combo niche.

2) Check your matchup spread: If the rising decks are bad matchups for you, adjust your sideboard or switch decks. If they are good matchups, your deck just got better.

3) Watch the first week of results: MTGO Challenges adapt fastest. Check MTGGoldfish for the new meta percentages before making changes.

4) Adjust sideboard first: Do not overhaul your main deck based on one week of data. Sideboard changes are low-risk and can be reversed.

5) Give it 3-4 weeks: The meta usually stabilizes after a month. Knee-jerk reactions often target last week's boogeyman while missing the actual new best deck.

What ban are you adapting to?

instruction: A new set just came out. How do I evaluate which cards might be Legacy-playable?
output:
Legacy card evaluation checklist:

1) Mana cost: Is it 0-2 mana? Cards above 2 mana need to be exceptional (Murktide at UU with delve, True-Name Nemesis at 1UU). 3+ mana cards need to end the game or provide overwhelming value.

2) Free or alternative cost: Can it be cast without paying mana? Force of Negation, Solitude, Endurance all see play because of pitch costs.

3) Does it pass the Bolt test? For creatures, does it survive Lightning Bolt (3+ toughness) or provide value even if Bolted (ETB effect)?

4) Does it fit an existing shell? Cards that slot into Delver, Show and Tell, D&T, or Stompy have a home immediately. Cards that need a new deck built around them rarely make it.

5) Does it compare favorably to what it replaces? Barrowgoyf replaced Tarmogoyf because it grows larger more efficiently. If the new card is not better than what already exists, it will not see play.

What cards are you evaluating?

instruction: My local meta is 30% Dimir Tempo, 20% combo, 20% Ocelot, 15% Stompy, 15% other. What should I play?
output:
With 30% Dimir Tempo, you want a deck that is favorable in that matchup since you will face it almost every round. Here is my analysis:

Best choice: Lands. Very favorable vs Dimir Tempo (your biggest matchup), even vs Ocelot, acceptable vs Stompy (depends on build), weak vs combo. Sideboard heavily for combo (4 Leyline of the Void, 2 Surgical). Expected win rate: 55-60% in this field.

Second choice: Sneak and Show. Favorable vs Dimir and Stompy, even vs Ocelot, races combo. More consistent matchup spread. Expected: 53-57%.

Third choice: Play Dimir Tempo yourself and win the mirror through skill and sideboard preparation. At 30% mirror, the player who knows the matchup better wins more. Expected: 50-55%.

Avoid: Burn (Bowmasters everywhere), Elves (Bowmasters and combo both destroy you), pure control (too slow for combo-heavy field).

Want me to build a specific list optimized for this meta?

instruction: Tell me about Brainstorm. Why is it the best card in Legacy?
output:
Brainstorm is a 1-mana instant that draws 3 cards and puts 2 back on top of your library. On its own, this is mediocre - you see 3 cards but keep the same number. What makes Brainstorm broken in Legacy is fetchlands.

The Brainstorm + fetchland interaction: Cast Brainstorm, draw 3 new cards, put 2 bad cards on top, then crack a fetchland to shuffle those bad cards away. You effectively drew the best card out of 4 for 1 mana at instant speed.

Additional uses: Hide important cards from Thoughtseize by putting them on top of your library. Put back a card you want to cast next turn. Set up Counterbalance or Delver of Secrets flips by choosing what goes on top.

Brainstorm is in 43% of Legacy decks. It is the single card that defines Legacy as a format. Playing blue in Legacy without Brainstorm requires a very good reason.

instruction: Is Force of Will actually good or just necessary?
output:
Both. Force of Will is a bad card in terms of card economy - you spend 2 cards (Force + pitch) to counter 1 card. In a fair, grindy game, this card disadvantage is real and painful.

But Force of Will is NECESSARY because without it, combo decks would dominate Legacy. Imagine a format where Storm, Oops All Spells, and Show and Tell have no free countermagic to worry about. Turn-1 kills would be the norm, and fair Magic would be unplayable.

Force is GOOD in specific contexts: protecting your own combo turn, stopping a game-winning spell, and in matchups where the tempo of the free counter matters more than the card disadvantage. Against combo, Force is excellent. Against midrange, it is often sideboarded out.

The skill of Legacy is knowing WHEN to Force. Forcing a Tarmogoyf is usually wrong. Forcing a Show and Tell is usually right. The decision point is: does this spell win the game if it resolves?

instruction: Why do people say Legacy is a Brainstorm format?
output:
Because Brainstorm shapes every aspect of Legacy deckbuilding and gameplay:

1) Deckbuilding: Every blue deck runs 4 Brainstorm and 8+ fetchlands to maximize the Brainstorm-shuffle interaction. This constrains mana bases toward fetchland-heavy configurations.

2) Card selection: Brainstorm means blue decks see more of their library than non-blue decks, finding answers and threats more consistently. This is why blue dominates Legacy.

3) Matchup knowledge: Because Brainstorm finds specific cards reliably, Legacy rewards knowing exactly which card matters in each matchup. A Brainstorm pilot who knows to find Surgical Extraction against Doomsday will win more than one who Brainstorms blindly.

4) Anti-Brainstorm strategies: Non-blue decks must either be fast enough that Brainstorm does not matter (combo, aggro), or disruptive enough to prevent Brainstorm from being effective (Chalice on 1, Narset Parter of Veils, Spirit of the Labyrinth).

5) The counterplay: Orcish Bowmasters punishes Brainstorm's card draw, which is why Bowmasters reshaped Legacy.

instruction: I want to build a deck around Urza's Saga. What are my options?
output:
Urza's Saga is one of the most versatile cards in Legacy. It creates Construct tokens that grow based on artifact count and tutors for 0-1 cost artifacts. Here are your options:

1) Affinity/8-Cast: Full artifact deck with Thought Monitor, Emry, and maximum Construct sizes. Tier 2 at 2.4%.
2) Painter: Saga tutors Grindstone (the combo piece) and makes Constructs for backup pressure. Tier 2 at 4.1%.
3) Mystic Forge Combo: Saga finds key combo pieces like Sensei's Divining Top (wait, banned). Instead finds Grinding Station or other 1-drops.
4) Death and Taxes: Some D&T builds splash Saga to tutor Pithing Needle, Retrofitter Foundry, or Shadowspear.
5) Grixis/UR Control: Saga as a value land in a fair blue shell.

Saga is in 22% of Legacy decks overall. What colors and strategy interest you?

instruction: I keep losing to graveyard decks. How do I fix my sideboard?
output:
The first question is: which graveyard decks are you losing to? They require different hate:

Vs Reanimator: Leyline of the Void (best, free), Surgical Extraction (targets specific creatures), Containment Priest (stops creatures entering without being cast).

Vs Dredge: Leyline of the Void (shuts off dredge entirely), Rest in Peace (same effect but costs 2 mana).

Vs Oops All Spells: Leyline of the Void (prevents the combo), Surgical Extraction (exile Thassa's Oracle).

Vs Doomsday: Surgical Extraction (exile Oracle or Doomsday from graveyard), Stifle (counter Oracle ETB).

The universal solution is 4 Leyline of the Void - it covers all of the above. If your deck uses its own graveyard (Murktide, delve), use Surgical Extraction or Endurance instead since Leyline would hurt you too. What deck are you playing?

instruction: My friend says I should play 4 Ponder in my Storm deck but I think Preordain is better there. Who is right?
output:
Your friend is right, and here is why: Ponder is better than Preordain in Storm for two critical reasons.

1) Ponder sees 3 cards and can shuffle if all 3 are bad. In Storm, you are looking for very specific cards (Lion's Eye Diamond, Burning Wish, Dark Ritual) and the ability to shuffle away 3 blanks is much more valuable than Preordain's scry 2 draw 1.

2) Ponder with a fetchland effectively sees 4 cards (3 from Ponder plus a fresh draw after shuffling). Storm needs to find combo pieces fast, and seeing more cards per cantrip is paramount.

3) Ponder also lets you stack the top of your library for Ad Nauseam (you want low-cost cards on top to draw more). Preordain cannot stack the deck.

The one case for Preordain: if you have no fetchlands (some budget builds), Preordain's scry is better than Ponder's 3-card look without shuffle.

instruction: Can you help me tune my Death and Taxes list for a combo-heavy meta?
output:
Absolutely. D&T's main weakness is combo, but you can shore it up significantly:

Maindeck changes:
- Run 4 Thalia, Guardian of Thraben (taxes every ritual and cantrip by 1)
- Add 2 Spirit of the Labyrinth (shuts off Brainstorm and cantrip chains)
- Consider 1-2 Ethersworn Canonist main (1 noncreature spell per turn wrecks Storm)
- Ensure 4 Rishadan Port (deny mana so combo cannot reach critical mana thresholds)

Sideboard:
- 3 Deafening Silence (1 noncreature spell per turn, devastating vs Storm and Oops)
- 2 Mindbreak Trap (free counter when they cast 3+ spells)
- 2 Containment Priest (stops Show and Tell, Reanimate, Sneak Attack)
- 1 Surgical Extraction (targets specific combo pieces)

The goal is not to out-combo them but to slow them down enough that your creatures kill them first. What combo decks are you specifically facing?

instruction: I opened a Volcanic Island in a collection I bought. What deck should I build around it?
output:
A Volcanic Island opens up several great deck options:

1) Izzet Delver (needs 2-3 Volcanic Island): Your opened copy is a great start. Add Delver, Murktide, Lightning Bolt, cantrips, Force of Will, Daze. Budget build possible with 1 real Volc + 1-2 Steam Vents. Cost to complete: $1500-2500.

2) Sneak and Show (needs 1-2 Volcanic Island): Volcanic provides the UR base for Show and Tell + Sneak Attack. Single Volcanic is enough with Ancient Tomb and Lotus Petal handling the rest. Cost to complete: $1500-2000.

3) Jeskai Stoneblade (needs 1-2 Volcanic Island + 2-3 Tundra): More expensive due to needing Tundra as well. Cost to complete: $2500-4000.

4) Ruby Storm (needs 0-1 Volcanic Island): Mostly mono-red but a Volcanic Island enables blue sideboard cards. Cheapest option at $400-700 to complete.

Volcanic Island is $500-700, so you have a $500+ head start. Which style interests you?

instruction: I am going to my first Legacy tournament this weekend. What should I know?
output:
Welcome to competitive Legacy! Here are the most important things:

1) Know the top 5 decks: Dimir Tempo (14.6%), Ocelot Pride (8.5%), Oops (8.2%), Sneak and Show (6.7%), Izzet Delver (4.3%). You WILL face at least 2-3 of these.

2) Mulligan decisions: Legacy mulligans are more punishing than other formats. Know what a keepable hand looks like for your deck and what you need to beat the popular matchups.

3) Sideboard plan: Write out your sideboard plan for each top 5 matchup BEFORE the tournament. Know what comes in and what goes out.

4) Play around Force of Will: 45% of decks play it. Do not run your best spell into open blue mana unless you have a backup plan.

5) Watch for Leyline of the Void: 36% of decks start with it in the sideboard. If you are on a graveyard deck, assume it exists.

6) Call a judge if unsure: Legacy has complex interactions. Judges are there to help. Never guess on a rules question.

What deck are you planning to play?

instruction: I played against Dimir Tempo three rounds in a row and lost every time. What am I doing wrong?
output:
Dimir Tempo is the most played deck for a reason, and losing to it is common. Let me diagnose the problem:

1) Are you playing into their Daze? If you tap out on turns 1-2, Daze punishes you for free. Play around it by making land drops and casting spells when you can pay the extra 1.

2) Are you feeding their Bowmasters? Every cantrip you cast past the first draw triggers Bowmasters. If they have BB open, consider whether that Brainstorm is worth giving them a 1/1 and a ping.

3) Are you using your removal correctly? Do not kill the Bowmasters 1/1 token - kill the Bowmasters itself. The Amass army grows with each trigger.

4) Are you sideboarding correctly? Pyroblast kills Murktide Regent for 1 mana. Toxic Deluge clears their board. Hydroblast counters their spells.

5) Are you on the right deck? Some decks simply have a bad Dimir Tempo matchup. If your deck folds to Thoughtseize + Force + Daze, consider switching to something that goes bigger (Lands, Sneak and Show).

What deck are you playing? I can give specific advice.

instruction: Someone told me Brainstorm is bad and I should play Preordain instead. Are they right?
output:
They are almost certainly wrong, assuming you are playing fetchlands. Brainstorm + fetchland is the most powerful cantrip interaction in Magic's history. Here is why:

Brainstorm alone: Draw 3, put back 2. Net effect: you see 3 new cards but keep the same hand size. Mediocre.

Brainstorm + fetchland: Draw 3, put 2 bad cards on top, crack fetchland, shuffle them away. Net effect: you drew the best of 4 cards and permanently removed 2 bad cards from the top of your deck. Exceptional.

Preordain: Scry 2 (look at 2 cards, put bad ones on bottom), draw 1. You see 3 cards total but can only bottom 2 of them. No shuffle synergy.

The ONLY case where Preordain is better: if you play zero fetchlands, no shuffle effects, and no library manipulation. In that case, Brainstorm's 2 cards put back on top are dead draws. But in Legacy, every blue deck plays 6-8 fetchlands, making this scenario irrelevant.

Brainstorm is the best cantrip in Legacy. Anyone saying otherwise does not understand the fetchland interaction.

instruction: Is Wasteland overpowered? Should it be banned?
output:
Wasteland is extremely powerful but should NOT be banned. Here is why it is actually healthy for the format:

1) Wasteland keeps greedy mana bases in check. Without it, every deck would play 4+ colors with zero deckbuilding cost. Wasteland forces you to run basic lands, which is a real constraint.

2) Wasteland enables non-blue strategies. Death and Taxes, Eldrazi, and Red Stompy all rely on mana denial to compete with cantrip-heavy blue decks. Without Wasteland, blue would be even more dominant.

3) Wasteland is answerable. You can play around it by fetching basics, running extra lands, or using mana creatures. Smart deckbuilding and play can minimize Wasteland's impact.

4) Wasteland enables interesting decisions. When to use it, what to target, and how to sequence your own lands around opponent's Wasteland are some of Legacy's most skill-testing decisions.

48% of decks play Wasteland. If it were banned, the entire format would need to be rebalanced. It is a format pillar, not a problem card.

instruction: Why was Deathrite Shaman banned? It seems like just a mana dork.
output:
Deathrite Shaman was far more than a mana dork. At the time of its ban in July 2018, it was in 57% of Legacy decks. Here is why it was broken:

1) It was a 1-mana creature that produced any color of mana by exiling lands from graveyards. In a format full of fetchlands, there were always lands to exile. This enabled effortless 3-4 color decks.

2) It gained 2 life by exiling instants/sorceries (relevant vs Burn and aggro).

3) It drained 2 life by exiling creatures (a win condition in grindy games and graveyard hate).

4) It had no deckbuilding cost - any deck with B or G could play it.

The result: 4-color midrange piles (Czech Pile) dominated the format because DRS fixed mana perfectly while providing utility. There was no reason NOT to play it. After the ban, the format diversified dramatically as 2-color tempo decks returned and mana bases required real choices again.

instruction: What is the deal with the Reserved List? Why can't they just reprint dual lands?
output:
The Reserved List is a promise Wizards of the Coast made in 1996 never to reprint certain cards. It was created after the Chronicles set reprinted many Legends cards, tanking their secondary market value and angering collectors.

The Reserved List includes all original dual lands (Underground Sea, Volcanic Island, etc.), Black Lotus, the Moxen, and hundreds of other old cards. Wizards has stated repeatedly that they will honor this commitment indefinitely.

For Legacy, this means: dual lands will never become cheaper through reprints. The supply is fixed and decreasing (cards get lost, damaged, or locked in collections). This is the primary barrier to Legacy accessibility and why the format is expensive.

The community is split: competitive players want abolition of the Reserved List for accessibility. Collectors and investors want it maintained to protect their holdings. Wizards treats it as legally binding regardless of community opinion.

Practical advice: buy duals if you can afford them (they appreciate in value), use shock lands if you cannot, and advocate for proxy-friendly events at your local store.

instruction: I want to build Lands but The Tabernacle at Pendrell Vale costs $5000. Is the deck playable without it?
output:
Lands is playable without Tabernacle but significantly weakened against creature-heavy strategies. Here is the honest assessment:

With Tabernacle: You have a land you can Crop Rotation for at instant speed that clears small creature boards (Elves, Goblins, D&T tokens) for free. It wins matchups single-handedly.

Without Tabernacle: You lose your best tool against go-wide creature strategies. Replacements like Pendrell Mists (4 mana enchantment) or Engineered Plague are much slower and less effective.

The practical path:
1) Build Lands without Tabernacle (~$3000-4000 with other Reserved List cards).
2) Play it and learn the deck. Tabernacle matters in maybe 20-30% of matchups.
3) Save for Tabernacle over time. It holds/appreciates in value.
4) In the meantime, use Crop Rotation to find Bojuka Bog, Karakas, or Ghost Quarter as utility lands instead.

Alternatively, consider Selesnya Depths ($800-1200) which uses the same Dark Depths combo in a creature-based shell without needing Tabernacle at all.

instruction: Can you design a Legacy deck that beats both Dimir Tempo and Oops All Spells?
output:
That is a tough ask because those two decks require very different answers. Dimir Tempo is a fair deck that grinds; Oops is an all-in combo. Here are decks that beat both:

1) Sneak and Show: Favorable vs Dimir Tempo (Emrakul dodges their removal, annihilator is devastating). Against Oops, you race with Force of Will backup. Post-board, Leyline of the Void handles Oops while Pyroblast handles Dimir. Win rate: ~55-60% vs both.

2) Lands: Favorable vs Dimir Tempo (Wasteland-proof, Maze of Ith, Punishing Fire). Against Oops, you rely on Bojuka Bog and sideboard Surgical Extraction. The Oops matchup is worse (maybe 40%), but Dimir is great (65%).

3) Painter: Fast combo races Oops or disrupts with Chalice. Against Dimir, Red Elemental Blast effects with Painter naming blue are unconditional counters. Win rate: ~55% vs both.

My recommendation: Sneak and Show for the most balanced matchup spread against both. Want me to build the list?

instruction: I play Dimir Tempo but I want to make it more unique. What spicy tech could I add?
output:
Love the brewer's mindset! Here are some spicy inclusions that most Dimir Tempo players do not run:

1) Subtlety (pitch for UU alternative): Puts a creature or planeswalker on top of their library instead of countering it. Surprise blowout when they tap out for a big threat.

2) Grief (wait, banned) - never mind. How about Unmask? Free hand disruption by exiling a black card. Worse than Thoughtseize most of the time but free is free.

3) Spell Snare: Counters 2-mana spells for U. Hits Bowmasters, Stoneforge Mystic, Counterbalance, Hymn to Tourach, Painter's Servant. Very meta-dependent.

4) Vendilion Clique: Flash 3/1 flyer that sees opponent's hand and can strip a card. Provides information for your countermagic decisions.

5) Brazen Borrower: Flash 3/1 flyer that bounces a permanent first. Handles resolved threats that Daze and Force missed.

6) Bitterblossom: Grindy enchantment that creates a 1/1 flyer every turn. Excellent in the mirror and against control. The life loss is manageable in tempo.

The key to spicy tech: it should not weaken your core matchups. Test each addition against Ocelot Pride, Oops, and Sneak and Show before committing. What sounds interesting?

instruction: I have $3000 to spend on Legacy. What is the best use of that money?
output:
With $3000 you have several strong options:

1) Full-power Ocelot Pride Midrange (~$2500): Tier 1 deck, no dual lands needed, competitive immediately. Leaves $500 for future upgrades or sideboard tuning.

2) Dimir Tempo with 2 Underground Sea + shock lands (~$2800): Get 2 real duals and fill the rest with Watery Grave. Functional at ~95% power. Upgrade to 3-4 Seas over time.

3) Sneak and Show (~$3000 with careful buying): Ancient Tomb, Show and Tell, Emrakul, Force of Will, cantrips, Lotus Petal. Skip the expensive Volcanic Islands initially.

4) Death and Taxes + Painter (~$2500 combined): Two competitive decks that share no cards, giving you metagame flexibility.

My recommendation: start with Ocelot Pride or Dimir Tempo. Both are tier 1, teach Legacy fundamentals, and their staples (Force of Will, fetchlands, Bowmasters) transfer to many other decks. What play style do you prefer?

instruction: How do I prepare for a specific matchup I keep losing to?
output:
Follow this process:

1) Identify WHY you are losing. Record your games or write notes. Are you losing to a specific card (Chalice, Blood Moon)? A specific game state (they combo before you can interact)? Or general card quality (they out-value you)?

2) Study the opposing deck. Read the archetype guide, understand their key cards, know their sideboard plan against you.

3) Adjust your sideboard. Dedicate 2-3 slots to your problem matchup. Bring in cards that directly answer their strategy.

4) Practice the matchup. Goldfish against their deck, play test games, learn the key decision points.

5) Adjust your play patterns. Maybe you are fetching wrong, Brainstorming at the wrong time, or using Force of Will on the wrong target.

6) Consider if the matchup is fixable. Some matchups are structurally terrible (Elves vs Bowmasters). If you cannot fix it with sideboard and play changes, accept it and focus on your good matchups.

What matchup are you struggling with?

instruction: My deck went 3-2 at a tournament. Two losses were to combo. How do I improve?
output:
3-2 is a solid start! Two combo losses suggests your disruption package or sideboard needs adjustment:

1) Check your maindeck interaction: Do you have 4 Force of Will? If not, that is problem #1. Are you running Thoughtseize or other proactive disruption? Against combo, reactive answers (Daze, Counterspell) are worse than proactive ones (Thoughtseize strips the combo piece before they can use it).

2) Check your sideboard: How many anti-combo cards do you have? With combo at ~40% of the meta, you want 5-7 sideboard slots dedicated to combo. Leyline of the Void (4), Flusterstorm (2), and Surgical Extraction (1) cover most bases.

3) Check your sideboard execution: Were you boarding correctly? Bring in ALL your combo hate, cut your weakest cards (usually creature removal against creatureless combo).

4) Check your play: Against combo, your #1 priority is disruption, #2 is clock. Deploy a threat AND hold up interaction. Do not tap out without Force backup.

What combo decks beat you, and what were the key moments?

instruction: I want to switch from Standard to Legacy. What should I know?
output:
The biggest differences from Standard:

1) Speed: Legacy games are often decided by turn 3-4. Turn-1 plays define the game. If your deck does not do something powerful by turn 2, you are behind.

2) Free spells: Force of Will, Daze, and pitch elementals (Solitude, Endurance) mean you are NEVER safe just because your opponent is tapped out. Always consider what free interaction they might have.

3) Brainstorm changes everything: 43% of decks play it. Card selection is so good that Legacy games feel like both players always have the right answer. This is because they often do.

4) The mana is perfect but fragile: Dual lands and fetchlands give flawless mana, but Wasteland and Blood Moon punish it. Always fetch basics early.

5) Sideboard matters more: With 54+ archetypes, your sideboard plan is different every round. Know your sideboard plans BEFORE the tournament.

6) Life total matters less: 20 life is often irrelevant because combo kills you regardless and fair decks end games through board state, not damage racing. The exception is Burn.

The format is incredibly deep and rewarding. Welcome!

instruction: Can you explain what a Daze check is?
output:
A Daze check is a play pattern where you cast a less important spell first to see if your opponent has Daze before committing your key spell.

Example: You have Delver of Secrets and Murktide Regent in hand, opponent has 1 untapped Island. Cast Delver first (the less important spell). If they Daze it, they bounced their Island - now they have ZERO lands and cannot interact with Murktide next turn. If they do not Daze, Delver resolves and you still have Murktide for later.

The concept: you are testing (checking) whether they have Daze by offering a bait spell. If they bite, you won the exchange. If they do not, your bait spell still resolved.

Advanced: experienced players know about Daze checks and may hold Daze for the more important spell. This creates a layer of mind games - do you lead with the card you want to resolve, or the bait? This is one of the deepest strategic elements in Legacy.

instruction: My friend says Legacy is dying. Is that true?
output:
No, Legacy is not dying, but it is evolving. The facts:

1) MTGO Legacy leagues fire constantly with healthy participation. The weekly Challenge events draw 32+ players and Showcase Qualifiers draw hundreds.

2) Paper Legacy has challenges due to Reserved List card prices, but local events thrive at stores that allow proxies.

3) The format receives active attention from Wizards with regular B&R updates. The March 2026 announcement specifically analyzed Legacy health.

4) Viewership for Legacy content on YouTube and Twitch is strong. Creators like Phil Gallagher, AnziD, and others have active communities.

5) MH3 and recent sets continue to print Legacy-impactful cards, keeping the format fresh.

The main barrier is cost, not player interest. Legacy has the most devoted player base of any format because the gameplay is unmatched. Proxy events, MTGO, and community organizations keep it accessible despite Reserved List prices.

instruction: What are the most common mistakes new Legacy players make?
output:
The top mistakes:

1) Brainstorming without a shuffle effect. If you cannot crack a fetchland after Brainstorm, you lock yourself with 2 bad cards on top.

2) Using Force of Will on the wrong spell. Do not Force a creature when you should Force the combo piece. Always ask: does this spell win the game if it resolves?

3) Not playing around Daze. If your opponent has an untapped Island and you tap out for a key spell, you deserve to get Dazed.

4) Fetching wrong. Fetching dual land on turn 1 instead of basic when you expect Wasteland is a common error that costs games.

5) Sideboarding incorrectly. Bringing in too many narrow cards or cutting core engine pieces (do not cut Brainstorm unless absolutely necessary).

6) Not knowing opponent's deck. Legacy has 54+ archetypes. If you do not know what your opponent is doing, you cannot make correct decisions.

7) Playing too slowly. Legacy rewards tight, efficient play. Every mana and card matters from turn 1. Durdle and you lose.

instruction: How does the London Mulligan work in Legacy?
output:
The London Mulligan: draw 7 cards, decide keep or mulligan. If you mulligan, draw 7 again, then put 1 card on the bottom of your library. Mulligan to 6: draw 7, put 1 on bottom (keep 6). Mulligan to 5: draw 7, put 2 on bottom (keep 5). And so on.

Legacy-specific considerations: 1) Combo decks mulligan aggressively because a 5-card hand with the combo is better than a 7-card hand without it. 2) Tempo decks keep loose hands more often because cantrips fix bad draws. 3) Hands with Brainstorm + fetchland can keep almost anything since Brainstorm sees 3 more cards. 4) Against unknown opponents game 1, keep hands that interact (Force of Will) over hands that are powerful but fragile. 5) After sideboard, you know the matchup - mulligan toward your hate cards.

instruction: What is the best way to learn Legacy?
output:
The optimal learning path:

1) Choose ONE deck and commit to it for at least 50 matches. Switching decks delays learning.

2) Learn your deck inside out: goldfish 20+ hands, write down your sideboard plans, know your matchup percentages.

3) Learn the top 10 opposing decks: know their key cards, how they win, what they sideboard, and what their tells are.

4) Study Brainstorm: if you play blue, learning when and how to Brainstorm is the single highest-impact skill. Watch expert players and notice when they Brainstorm vs hold it.

5) Play on MTGO for volume. Paper events are great for practice but MTGO provides 10x the match volume.

6) Watch content: streamers explain their decisions in real-time. Pause and think about what you would do before seeing their play.

7) Review your own games: what did you miss? What could you have played differently? The best players constantly self-critique.

The biggest mistake new players make is trying to learn the entire format at once instead of mastering one deck first.

instruction: I just lost a match where my opponent played cards I have never seen before. How do I handle unknown decks?
output:
Unknown decks are common in Legacy with 54+ archetypes. Here is how to handle them:

1) Identify the macro-strategy: are they combo (trying to win in one big turn), tempo (cheap threats + disruption), control (answering everything), or prison (lock pieces)? This tells you your general approach.

2) Look for format staples: if they play Force of Will, they are blue and interactive. If they play Ancient Tomb, they are prison/stompy. If they play Dark Ritual, they are combo.

3) Default to safe play: keep Force of Will for anything that looks game-winning. Do not use Wasteland on random lands unless you are sure.

4) Ask after the match: Legacy players are generally happy to explain their deck. Learn what it does so you are prepared next time.

5) After the tournament, look up the deck on MTGGoldfish or the archetype guide. Add it to your mental database of 'decks I know.'

The advantage of experience: after playing Legacy for a year, you will recognize almost every deck from the first 1-2 cards played.

instruction: I drew Brainstorm but I have no fetchland. Should I still cast it?
output:
Usually no, unless you have a specific reason. Brainstorm without a shuffle effect (fetchland, Ponder) means you put 2 cards on top and draw them over the next 2 turns. This is called a 'Brainstorm lock' - you already know your next 2 draws and they are the cards you did not want.

Cast Brainstorm without a fetch when: 1) You are about to die and need to see 3 new cards RIGHT NOW. 2) You need to hide a card from Thoughtseize (put it on top before they see your hand). 3) You have another shuffle effect coming (Ponder next turn). 4) You want to put a card on top for Counterbalance or Delver flip.

Otherwise, hold Brainstorm until you have a fetchland available. The power of Brainstorm is the shuffle, and casting it without one wastes most of its value.

instruction: Explain the Legacy metagame clock: what determines how fast decks need to be.
output:
The Legacy metagame clock is set by the fastest combo decks:

1) Oops All Spells can win turn 1. This means every deck needs a plan for turn 1 interaction (Force of Will, Daze) or risk dying before playing.

2) Storm and Sneak and Show reliably win turns 2-3. This sets the 'goldfish clock' - if your deck cannot either win or meaningfully interact by turn 3, it is too slow.

3) Dimir Tempo deploys threats turn 1-2 and applies disruption + pressure simultaneously. Fair decks must match this efficiency or be run over.

Consequences: Legacy decks are hyper-efficient because anything slow gets killed by combo. This is why 1-mana spells dominate, why free counterspells are essential, and why 4+ mana cards need to win the game immediately. The metagame clock also explains why control is viable: it trades speed for inevitability, using free countermagic to survive the early turns.

instruction: My opponent mulliganed to 5 cards. How does that change my strategy?
output:
Your opponent mulliganing to 5 means they kept a focused hand with a specific plan. Adjust accordingly:

1) If they are combo: they likely kept a hand that can combo but has minimal protection. Be aggressive with disruption - Thoughtseize them immediately. Their 5 cards might be exactly the combo with no backup.

2) If they are fair: they are behind on resources. Press your card advantage. Every 1-for-1 trade favors you because they have fewer cards. Do not let them stabilize.

3) If they are prison: they might have kept a hand with a lock piece and fast mana. Be ready with Force of Will for their turn 1 play - a 5-card prison hand is all-in on one lock piece.

4) General: do not mulligan your own hand aggressively just because they mulliganed. Your 7 cards beats their 5 in almost any scenario. Keep a reasonable hand and ride the card advantage.

instruction: I am thinking about buying into Legacy. Convince me it is worth it.
output:
Here is why Legacy is worth the investment:

1) The best gameplay in Magic. Legacy has the deepest card pool, the highest skill ceiling, and the most diverse metagame. Every game involves meaningful decisions from turn 1.

2) Your deck never rotates. A Legacy deck bought today will be legal forever (barring specific card bans). Standard decks become worthless every 2 years. Legacy decks are permanent.

3) Financial appreciation. Reserved List cards (dual lands, LED) only go up in value. Your Underground Seas will be worth more in 5 years than today. Legacy is the only Magic format where your collection consistently appreciates.

4) Community. Legacy players are the most passionate and knowledgeable in Magic. The community is tight-knit, welcoming to new players, and deeply invested in the format's health.

5) Accessibility is improving. MTGO lets you play for $300-500. Many stores allow proxies. You do not need $5000 on day one.

6) Unmatched variety. 54 competitive archetypes mean you can always find something new to play or play against. No two weeks of Legacy feel the same.

instruction: Walk me through the thought process of building a Legacy sideboard from scratch.
output:
Step-by-step sideboard construction:

1) List your worst 5-8 matchups. For Dimir Tempo: Lands, Oops, Storm, Sneak and Show, Red Stompy, Ocelot Pride.

2) For each matchup, identify the 1-2 cards that would improve it most. Lands: Blood Moon or Surgical on Loam. Oops: Leyline of the Void. Storm: Flusterstorm. Sneak and Show: Pyroblast. Red Stompy: Force of Vigor. Ocelot: Toxic Deluge.

3) Find overlap. Leyline covers Oops AND Dredge AND Doomsday. Pyroblast covers S&T AND Delver mirrors. Flusterstorm covers Storm AND counter wars. Maximize cards that help in multiple matchups.

4) Allocate slots. Start with your most common bad matchups. If Oops is 8.2% and you face it every other tournament, 4 Leylines is justified. If Dredge is 1.1%, 0 dedicated Dredge hate is fine (Leyline covers it anyway).

5) Final 15: 4 Leyline of the Void, 2 Flusterstorm, 2 Pyroblast, 2 Hydroblast, 2 Toxic Deluge, 2 Null Rod, 1 Surgical Extraction. Every slot improves at least 2 matchups.

6) Test. Play 10 matches with sideboard, adjust based on what you actually board in/out.

instruction: My opponent just revealed Leyline of the Void from their opening hand. I am on Doomsday. What do I do?
output:
Do not panic. Leyline is painful but not game over for Doomsday:

1) Your win condition (Thassa's Oracle) checks LIBRARY SIZE, not graveyard. Oracle says 'if X is greater than or equal to cards in library' where X is devotion to blue. Leyline does not affect this.

2) The problem: your cantrips send cards to exile instead of graveyard, so you cannot use graveyard-based pile navigation. But you can still cast Doomsday (it resolves normally), and draw through your 5-card pile with cantrips.

3) Your pile: needs to draw through itself using only draw spells, not graveyard interaction. Example pile: Brainstorm, Ponder, Oracle, Force of Will, blue card. Cast Brainstorm (draw Ponder, Oracle, Force, put 2 back), cast Ponder (see remaining cards, draw Oracle), cast Oracle with 0 cards in library.

4) Alternatively: find and cast Force of Vigor (pitch green card) to destroy Leyline first, then proceed with a normal graveyard-enabled pile.

The key: Leyline slows you down by 1-2 turns but does not prevent the kill. Stay calm and construct your pile carefully.

instruction: What questions should I ask myself before every Legacy game?
output:
Pre-game mental checklist:

1) What deck is my opponent on? (If game 1, infer from their first plays. If game 2-3, you know.)

2) What is their fastest possible kill? (Turn 1 for Oops, turn 2-3 for Storm, turn 3-4 for fair decks.) This sets your urgency level.

3) What is MY role in this matchup? (Aggressor, controller, or combo player?) This determines whether you deploy threats or hold interaction.

4) What card in their deck do I lose to? (Emrakul, Blood Moon, Chalice, etc.) This tells you what to Force of Will.

5) What is my mulligan threshold? (Keep any hand with Force against combo. Keep any hand with 2 lands + cantrip against fair.)

6) What is my sideboard plan? (Know BEFORE you sit down what comes in and what goes out.)

7) What is their sideboard plan against me? (Are they bringing in Leyline? Pyroblast? Chalice? Adjust your play accordingly.)

Asking these questions before every game takes 30 seconds and dramatically improves your decision-making.

instruction: What should I do between rounds at a Legacy tournament?
output: Between rounds: 1) Write down your opponent's deck and any unusual cards you saw. This helps future matchup preparation. 2) Review the match: what decisions could you have made differently? 3) Eat and hydrate. Tournaments are long and mental fatigue causes mistakes. 4) Check standings if available - knowing your likely opponents helps plan ahead. 5) Do NOT obsess over a loss. Reset mentally for the next match. 6) If time permits, watch a match involving a deck you might face to scout their list. 7) Review your sideboard plan for the most likely next-round opponent. The players who perform best at tournaments are the ones who stay focused and mentally fresh throughout the day.

instruction: My friend and I want to playtest Legacy but we only own two decks. How do we get the most out of testing?
output:
Maximize two-deck testing with this structure:

1) Play 10 pre-board games. Track who wins each one. This establishes your game 1 matchup.

2) Develop sideboard plans for both sides. Write them down.

3) Play 10 post-board games. Track results. Compare to game 1 results.

4) Switch decks and repeat. Playing both sides teaches you what the opponent is trying to do, which improves your play when you switch back.

5) After 20+ games each way, discuss: what were the key cards? What lines won the most? What sideboard cards overperformed or underperformed?

6) Adjust decklists and repeat.

Even with just 2 decks, this deep matchup knowledge is more valuable than superficial experience across 10 decks. You will know this matchup better than anyone at a tournament.

instruction: I want to build a Legacy gauntlet for testing. What decks should I include?
output:
A minimum viable Legacy gauntlet covers the top macro-strategies:

1) Dimir Tempo (tier 1 tempo, 14.6%): the deck to beat
2) Oops All Spells (tier 1 fast combo, 8.2%): tests your combo interaction
3) Sneak and Show (tier 1 midrange combo, 6.7%): tests your ability to stop resolved permanents
4) Ocelot Pride (tier 1 aggro/midrange, 8.5%): tests your creature interaction
5) Red Stompy (tier 2 prison, 2.7%): tests your ability to play through Chalice and Blood Moon
6) Death and Taxes (tier 2 hatebears, 2.0%): tests your ability to fight through taxes

These 6 decks cover: tempo, fast combo, midrange combo, aggro, prison, and hatebears - representing ~42% of the total metagame. If your deck has positive matchups against all 6, it is well-positioned.

instruction: How do I learn to play Brainstorm correctly?
output:
Brainstorm mastery comes in stages:

Stage 1 (beginner): Cast Brainstorm, put back 2 bad cards, crack fetchland. This is correct 80% of the time.

Stage 2 (intermediate): Think before casting. Ask: do I have a fetchland? What am I looking for? Is now the right time? Hold Brainstorm if you do not have a shuffle effect.

Stage 3 (advanced): Consider what to put back in the context of the matchup. Against Thoughtseize, put important cards on TOP (they cannot take them). Consider putting back cards that are good later but not now (a sweeper you do not need yet).

Stage 4 (expert): Brainstorm in response to specific triggers. Brainstorm in response to Thoughtseize to hide cards. Brainstorm before your draw step to set up Delver flips. Brainstorm to put back a card you want to Daze (return Island, draw the card you put back).

The difference between a good and great Legacy player is often just Brainstorm decisions. Play 100+ games focusing specifically on Brainstorm timing before moving on to other skills.

instruction: What is the etiquette for Legacy tournaments?
output:
Legacy community norms:

1) Be friendly and discuss games after the match. Legacy players love talking about the format.

2) Announce triggers clearly, especially Bowmasters, Delver flips, and Vial counters. Missing triggers in competitive play can result in judge calls.

3) Do not slow play. Legacy has complex board states but players are expected to play at a reasonable pace. If you are thinking for a long time, say so.

4) Shuffle your opponent's deck after they search. This is standard procedure.

5) Call a judge if you are unsure about a rules interaction. Judges are neutral arbiters and calling one is NOT rude.

6) Do not scoop early if your opponent needs practice playing through a win. Many players appreciate seeing the combo or kill in action.

7) If playing with proxies (at stores that allow them), make sure they are clearly readable and distinguishable. Good proxy etiquette means your opponent can easily read your cards.

instruction: What is the most satisfying play in Legacy?
output:
Every Legacy player has their own answer, but the community favorites are:

1) Brainstorm in response to Thoughtseize: hide your 2 best cards on top of your library, then the opponent sees a hand of your worst cards. Pure skill expression.

2) Turn 1 kill: Any deck that wins on turn 1 (Oops, Belcher, Storm) produces an electrifying moment. Laying out the combo and watching the opponent realize they are dead is Legacy at its most dramatic.

3) Natural Order into Craterhoof for lethal: sacrificing a tiny 1/1 elf and replacing it with a 5/5 that gives your 8 other elves +9/+9 trample for a 70+ damage attack.

4) Perfect Doomsday pile: constructing a 5-card pile that plays through opponent's disruption, draws through itself, and wins with Oracle. The intellectual satisfaction is unmatched.

5) Flipping Delver on turn 2: the iconic Legacy moment. Revealing Brainstorm or Force of Will and attacking for 3 in the air.

What is yours?

instruction: My local game store is starting a Legacy night. How do I help grow the community?
output:
Growing a Legacy community takes effort but pays off:

1) Advocate for proxy-friendly events. The #1 barrier to Legacy is cost. Allowing proxies lets players try the format without $3000+ investment.

2) Lend decks. If you own multiple Legacy decks, let new players borrow one. One good experience hooks them.

3) Teach while you play. Explain interactions, suggest plays, and do not punish new player mistakes harshly. Legacy has a steep learning curve and new players need encouragement.

4) Start a Discord or group chat for your local Legacy players. Coordinate events, share decklists, and discuss the meta.

5) Record and share your matches (with permission). Content helps attract players who are Legacy-curious.

6) Be consistent. Show up every week. Community events die when attendance is unreliable.

7) Run beginner-friendly events with simplified rules (maybe best-of-1 with premade decks) to introduce the format.

The Legacy community is the best in Magic because the players care deeply about the format. Be part of that tradition.

instruction: What should I read to get better at Legacy?
output:
Essential reading and watching:

1) Articles: MTGGoldfish's 'This Week in Legacy' (weekly meta analysis), Legacy articles on ChannelFireball and TCGPlayer by Reid Duke, Anuraag Das, and others.

2) Primers: The Source (mtgsalvation.com) has dedicated threads for every archetype maintained over years. Read the primer for YOUR deck cover to cover.

3) Videos: Phil Gallagher (PhilGallagher on YouTube) plays and explains Legacy matches. AnziD streams Legacy on Twitch with excellent commentary. Julian Knab provides high-level Storm content.

4) Books: 'LLMs in Production' covers the AI aspects of building Legacy tools (this project's course textbook).

5) Reddit: r/MTGLegacy is active with meta discussion, deck help, and card evaluation.

6) Discord: Multiple Legacy Discord servers for specific archetypes and general discussion.

The fastest improvement comes from playing + reviewing your own matches. But reading and watching helps you learn what good play looks like before you can execute it yourself.

instruction: I am about to play my first Legacy game ever. What is the one most important thing to remember?
output:
Fetch basic lands first.

Seriously. The single most impactful advice for a new Legacy player is: when you crack a fetchland turn 1, get a basic Island (or whatever basic your deck needs) instead of a dual land. Here is why:

1) 48% of Legacy decks play Wasteland. If your only land is a dual land, they destroy it and you have nothing.

2) Blood Moon (in 10-15% of decks) turns all nonbasics into Mountains. A basic Island still works under Blood Moon.

3) You can always fetch the dual later when you need both colors.

The number of games lost by new Legacy players because they fetched Underground Sea turn 1 and got Wastelanded is incalculable. Fetch. Basic. First. Everything else can be learned over time, but this one habit will immediately improve your win rate.

instruction: I just went 0-4 at a Legacy tournament. Should I switch decks?
output:
Not necessarily. 0-4 in a single tournament can happen with any deck due to variance. Before switching, ask:

1) Were your losses due to bad matchups, bad draws, or bad play? If matchups: maybe your deck is poorly positioned in your local meta. If draws: variance, play more. If play: focus on improving, not switching.

2) How many total Legacy matches have you played with this deck? If fewer than 30, you are still learning. Give it more time.

3) Did you enjoy playing the deck? Enjoyment matters more than a single tournament result. Legacy is a long-term commitment.

4) Review each loss specifically. Write down the key moments. Often you will find 2-3 decisions that cost you games.

Only switch if: you consistently (30+ matches) have a sub-45% win rate AND you have identified structural matchup problems that sideboarding cannot fix. One bad tournament is noise, not signal.

instruction: How do I deal with tilt in Legacy?
output:
Legacy tilt is especially brutal because the format's complexity means mistakes compound. Tips:

1) Take a breath between games. Literally. 10 seconds of breathing resets your mental state.

2) Focus on the next decision, not the last mistake. You cannot undo a bad Brainstorm, but you can play the next turn optimally.

3) If you just lost a match: stand up, get water, walk around for 2 minutes before the next round. Physical movement breaks the tilt cycle.

4) Remember that every player, including world champions, makes mistakes and has losing streaks. One session does not define your skill.

5) Avoid results-oriented thinking. A correct play that loses is still correct. An incorrect play that wins is still incorrect. Judge your play quality, not the outcome.

6) If you are genuinely tilted after 3 rounds, consider dropping and playing casually. Forcing yourself through rounds while tilted just compounds bad decisions.

Legacy is a marathon, not a sprint.

instruction: Should I practice Legacy against AI or only against humans?
output:
Both have value:

AI practice (goldfishing, simple bots): Best for: learning your own deck's sequencing, practicing opening hands, memorizing sideboard plans, and understanding your average goldfish speed. Goldfishing 20 hands teaches you more about your deck's consistency than 5 matches against humans. Weakness: AI does not play like humans, so you do not learn interaction timing.

Human practice: Best for: learning matchup dynamics, practicing Brainstorm decisions under pressure, sideboard evolution, and developing metagame intuition. You cannot learn Daze math or Force of Will decisions against AI. Weakness: slower, dependent on finding opponents.

Optimal split: 30% goldfishing/AI (learn your deck mechanics), 70% human practice (learn the format). For your first 10 matches with a new deck, goldfish 80% of the time to learn sequencing before adding opponent interaction.

instruction: What makes Legacy the best format in Magic?
output: Legacy players will tell you: 1) Every card in Magic's history is available. The deepest card pool means the most interesting deckbuilding decisions. 2) Skill expression is unmatched. Brainstorm alone has hundreds of decision trees. The gap between a good and great Legacy player is wider than any other format. 3) No rotation. Your deck investment lasts forever. A deck built in 2015 (with updates) is still competitive in 2026. 4) Community. Legacy players are passionate, knowledgeable, and welcoming. The format's barrier to entry creates a self-selecting community of dedicated players. 5) Game diversity. With 54+ archetypes, every tournament round is different. You face combo, tempo, control, and prison all in one event. 6) History. Playing with Alpha cards alongside modern designs connects you to Magic's entire 30+ year history. Legacy is not just a format - it is Magic at its most complete.

instruction: Convince me to try Legacy if I have never played an eternal format.
output:
Try Legacy because:

1) You will play Magic at its most powerful. Cards that defined every era - Brainstorm, Force of Will, Lightning Bolt, Swords to Plowshares - all in one format.

2) You do not need to spend $5000 to start. MTGO costs $300-500. Many stores allow proxies. Burn is $1500 in paper.

3) The learning curve is steep but the payoff is enormous. Once you understand Legacy's interactions, every other format feels shallow by comparison.

4) Your collection appreciates. Reserved List cards only go up. This is the only Magic format where your cards are an investment.

5) The community will welcome you. Legacy players WANT more people to play the format. Ask questions, borrow decks, and people will help.

6) Start with one deck and master it. You do not need to know every card on day 1. Brainstorm and Force of Will will teach you the rest.

The first time you Brainstorm in response to Thoughtseize to hide your best cards, you will understand why Legacy players never switch formats.

instruction: I want to teach my friend to play Legacy. Where should we start?
output:
Start with a simple matchup between two straightforward decks:

Beginner pair: Burn vs Mono Red Stompy. Both are mono-color, have clear game plans, and do not require Brainstorm decisions. Burn teaches damage math and sequencing. Stompy teaches prison strategy and threat deployment.

Intermediate pair: Izzet Delver vs Death and Taxes. This teaches cantrip sequencing, Daze math, Aether Vial timing, and sideboarding. Both decks have clear roles.

Teach in stages: 1) Start with goldfish (just play your own deck). 2) Then play game 1s only (no sideboarding). 3) Then add sideboards. 4) Then discuss specific Brainstorm lines.

Key: do not start with combo decks. Learning Legacy through Storm or Doomsday is overwhelming. The format fundamentals (cantrips, fetchlands, Force of Will) are best learned through fair decks.

instruction: What are the most common mistakes when sideboards are built for Legacy?
output: 1) Too many singletons: 15 different cards means you rarely draw any specific one. Better to have 3-4 copies of your most important hate cards. 2) Cards that do not come in for any specific matchup: if you cannot name 2+ matchups where a card improves your win rate, cut it. 3) Hate that is too narrow: Tormod's Crypt ONLY hits graveyards. Endurance hits graveyards AND is a 3/4 body AND pitches to Force of Vigor. Choose flexible cards. 4) Not adjusting for your local meta: online sideboards are built for the MTGO meta. Your local meta may be 50% combo, requiring 8 combo hate slots instead of 5. 5) Forgetting to test sideboard games: most players test game 1s and guess at sideboard plans. Test post-board games too.

instruction: What format should I try after Legacy?
output:
If you love Legacy, you will probably enjoy: 1) Vintage: even more powerful card pool, Power 9 unrestricted... wait, they are restricted. The ultimate power fantasy with Moxen and Ancestral Recall at 1 copy each. Very small community. 2) Old School (93/94): only cards from 1993-1994. Pure nostalgia and the origins of Magic. 3) Canadian Highlander: 100-card singleton with a points system for powerful cards. Combines Legacy's power with deck diversity. 4) Commander/cEDH: multiplayer, uses many Legacy cards. cEDH is competitive and has a Legacy-like feel. 5) Modern: more accessible, less powerful, but still a non-rotating format with good gameplay.

Most Legacy players also play Commander casually and occasionally Modern. Very few quit Legacy for another format - the gameplay is too good.

instruction: How has Legacy changed since I last played in 2019?
output:
Massive changes since 2019:

Banned since 2019: Wrenn and Six (2019), Underworld Breach (2020), Arcum's Astrolabe (2020), Ragavan (2022), Expressive Iteration (2023), White Plume Adventurer (2023), Grief (2024), Nadu (2024), Psychic Frog (2025), Entomb (2025).

Format-defining additions: Orcish Bowmasters (2023, most played creature), Murktide Regent (2021, premier threat), Urza's Saga (2021, artifact enabler), The One Ring (2023, card advantage engine), energy cards from MH3 (2024, created non-blue aggro).

Meta shift: 2019 was UR Delver dominance with Wrenn and Six. 2026 is Dimir Tempo (14.6%) with diverse combo. The format is healthier and more diverse than 2019. Bowmasters fundamentally changed how cantrips are used. Energy cards made non-blue competitive for the first time in years.

instruction: Is it too late to start playing Legacy in 2026?
output:
Absolutely not. It is actually a great time to start:

1) The format is the healthiest it has been in years: 54 archetypes, balanced meta, no oppressive deck.

2) Budget entry points exist: Oops ($1100), Burn ($1500), MTGO ($300-500). You do not need dual lands on day 1.

3) Proxy acceptance is growing. More stores allow proxied Legacy events, removing the cost barrier.

4) MTGO provides unlimited practice at low cost.

5) The player base is stable and welcoming. Legacy communities actively recruit new players because growing the format benefits everyone.

6) Reserved List cards appreciate, so your investment grows over time.

The only argument against starting Legacy is the cost, and there are now multiple ways to play competitively without the full investment. Start on MTGO or at a proxy-friendly store. Master one deck. Then decide if you want to invest in paper. By the time you are ready for paper, you will know the format well enough to make smart purchasing decisions.

instruction: What is the biggest misconception about Legacy?
output: The biggest misconception is that Legacy is 'turn 1 combo format where nothing else matters.' In reality, Legacy is the most diverse and interactive format in Magic. Yes, combo EXISTS and can win turn 1, but: 1) Force of Will in 45% of decks checks combo. 2) The most played deck (Dimir Tempo) is a fair tempo strategy. 3) Creature combat, planeswalkers, and grinding are central to most games. 4) Combo represents about 35-40% of the meta, meaning 60-65% is fair Magic. The average Legacy game involves interactive decisions every turn, card selection through cantrips, and strategic resource management. It is not two ships passing in the night.

instruction: How do I explain Legacy to someone who only plays Standard?
output: Legacy is Standard but with every card ever printed and everything costs 1 mana. Imagine if your Standard deck could play the most efficient version of every effect: removal for 1 mana (Swords to Plowshares), countermagic for free (Force of Will), card draw for 1 mana (Brainstorm), and threats for 1-2 mana that would be banned in Standard. The result: games are faster, decisions are denser, and every turn matters from the very first. Think of it like chess vs checkers: Standard has simpler pieces moving on a simpler board. Legacy has more pieces, more possible moves, and more ways to win or lose on any given turn.

instruction: Why do Legacy players love the format so much?
output: Because Legacy offers what no other format can: 1) Intellectual depth - Brainstorm alone has more decision trees than most entire Standard formats. 2) Personal expression - with 54 archetypes, every player finds a deck that matches their personality. 3) History - playing with Alpha duals alongside modern cards connects you to Magic's entire history. 4) Mastery reward - the format rewards deep knowledge more than any other. A Legacy master consistently beats players on better decks through superior play. 5) Community - the shared barrier to entry creates a community of passionate, committed players who genuinely care about each other and the format. 6) Permanence - your deck never rotates. The skills, cards, and knowledge you build last forever.

instruction: What would you tell someone who just lost their first 5 Legacy matches?
output:
Welcome to Legacy. Losing your first matches is NORMAL and expected. Here is why you should not be discouraged:

1) Legacy has the steepest learning curve in Magic. Every opponent knows their deck intimately. You are competing against years of accumulated knowledge.

2) Your losses are educational. Each one teaches you an interaction, a timing window, or a card you need to know. Write down what beat you.

3) Brainstorm decisions take months to optimize. If you are playing blue, your cantrip skills will improve dramatically over the next 50 games.

4) Sideboarding improves with experience. You will learn which cards to bring in once you see what opponents are doing.

5) Every Legacy player went through this. The difference between you and them is 100-500 more games of practice.

Commit to 30 more games with the same deck before evaluating. Legacy does not reveal its depth in 5 matches.

instruction: What is the most important skill to develop for Legacy?
output: Brainstorm timing and execution. Brainstorm is in 43% of Legacy decks and is cast multiple times per game. The difference between casting Brainstorm well and casting it poorly is the difference between winning and losing. Key Brainstorm skills: 1) When to cast (have a fetchland? what are you looking for? is now the right time?). 2) What to put back (context-dependent: hide from Thoughtseize, set up Delver, put back situational cards). 3) When to hold (no fetchland = no shuffle = bad Brainstorm). Master Brainstorm and you master Legacy. Everything else - Daze math, Force timing, sideboarding - is secondary to cantrip proficiency.

instruction: I love Grixis colors but I am not sure what Legacy deck to play. What are my options?
output: Grixis has some great options in Legacy! Grixis Delver/Tempo plays Delver of Secrets alongside Orcish Bowmasters, Murktide Regent, and Thoughtseize with red for Lightning Bolt and sideboard Pyroblast. Grixis Scam uses evoked Grief with Not Dead After All for devastating turn-1 plays. Grixis Control takes a slower approach with Kolaghan's Command and planeswalkers. What is your play style preference: fast tempo, explosive starts, or long grindy games?

instruction: What Legacy deck has the best matchup against Dimir Tempo?
output: Several decks match up well against Dimir Tempo. Eldrazi Stompy is excellent because Chalice on 1 shuts off their cantrips and Thought-Knot Seer strips their best cards. Lands is strong because Dimir Tempo cannot interact with the Dark Depths combo easily. Painter has a good matchup because Red Elemental Blast effects overload their countermagic. Would you like to know more about any of these options?

instruction: I pulled a Volcanic Island from a booster. What Legacy deck should I build around it?
output: Congratulations on the pull! Volcanic Island opens up several powerful archetypes. Izzet Delver is the most natural home - it plays Delver of Secrets, Murktide Regent, Lightning Bolt, and the blue cantrip shell. You would need approximately 3 more Volcanic Islands, but some lists function with 2-3 plus basics. Alternatively, Sneak and Show uses 2-3 Volcanic Islands alongside its combo. Jeskai Control is another option. What is your budget for the rest of the deck?

instruction: I have been playing Dimir Tempo but want something different. What should I try next?
output: Since you already know the blue cantrip shell, you have a great foundation! If you want to stay tempo but change colors, Izzet Delver gives you Lightning Bolt and Dragon's Rage Channeler for a more aggressive angle. For a combo shift, Doomsday uses the same Brainstorm-Ponder-Force shell but wins deterministically with Doomsday piles. For something completely different, Painter is a fun artifact combo deck that attacks from a unique angle. What aspect of Dimir Tempo do you enjoy most?

instruction: How do I decide between Daze and Spell Pierce for my Legacy deck?
output: Great question - they serve different roles. Daze is better in aggressive tempo decks (Delver, Dimir Tempo) that apply early pressure and want free interaction while tapped out deploying threats. The land-bounce cost matters less when you are winning fast. Spell Pierce is better in control and midrange decks that plan to go longer, where Daze's land bounce is too costly. In the late game, Spell Pierce often taxes 2 effectively while Daze is just bad. Many decks play both in some split.

instruction: Should I play Mono-Red Prison or Eldrazi Stompy? They seem similar.
output: They share the prison shell (Chalice, fast mana, Sol lands) but differ in meaningful ways. Mono-Red Prison has Blood Moon and Trinisphere for harder locks, plus Goblin Rabblemaster and Fable of the Mirror-Breaker for grinding. Eldrazi Stompy has Thought-Knot Seer for hand disruption and Reality Smasher as a 5/5 haste threat that is hard to remove. Red Prison is better against 3+ color decks (Blood Moon). Eldrazi is better against 1-2 color fair decks. What does your local meta look like?

instruction: I want to play a Legacy deck that uses the graveyard but is not Reanimator. What are my options?
output: There are several graveyard strategies beyond Reanimator! Dredge uses the dredge mechanic to mill your library and win with Ichorid, Narcomoeba, and Bridge from Below. Hogaak runs Hogaak, Arisen Necropolis and Vengevine for recursive creature pressure. Lands uses Life from the Loam to recur Wastelands endlessly. Oops All Spells mills your entire library turn 1 for the win. Each has a different relationship with the graveyard - which appeals to you?

instruction: How important is it to own original dual lands for Legacy?
output: Dual lands are important but not strictly required to compete. Shock lands (Watery Grave, Steam Vents) are functional replacements that cost 2 life per untapped use. The life cost matters most against Burn and aggressive strategies. For your first Legacy deck, consider archetypes that need fewer duals: Oops All Spells needs zero, Burn needs zero, Eldrazi needs zero, and Painter needs 0-1. As you grow your collection, you can upgrade to duals over time. What deck are you considering?

instruction: I am coming from Standard. How different is Legacy?
output: Legacy is dramatically faster and more interactive than Standard. Games can end on turns 1-3 with combo decks, and free spells like Force of Will, Daze, and evoke creatures mean no one is ever safe. Card selection is incredible with Brainstorm and Ponder. Sideboards matter enormously because hate cards are more powerful. The format rewards format knowledge over raw card power. I would recommend starting with a proactive strategy like Burn or Elves to learn the format. What colors do you enjoy in Standard?

instruction: Can I play Legacy on Magic Online? What is the meta like there?
output: Yes, MTGO has an active Legacy community with daily Challenges, Leagues, and Showcase events. The online meta tends to be slightly more combo-heavy because experienced combo pilots gravitate to MTGO. Dimir Tempo dominates online as well. Deck prices are much cheaper online - Underground Sea is around $20-30 instead of $600+, making full-power lists accessible. I would recommend starting with Leagues (5 matches) to learn the format. What deck are you interested in trying?

instruction: I keep losing to Orcish Bowmasters. What can I do?
output: Bowmasters punishes card draws, so first minimize unnecessary cantripping. Play Brainstorm only when you have a reason, not just to dig. Card-specific answers include: Swords to Plowshares (exiles it cleanly), Pyroblast (it is black, not blue, so this does not work - use other removal), Dress Down (removes its ability), and Toxic Deluge (clears both Bowmasters and the army token). Strategically, play threats that outclass it: Murktide Regent, True-Name Nemesis, and Marit Lage all ignore Bowmasters. What deck are you playing?

instruction: What is the best first Legacy deck for someone who loves control?
output: For a control-oriented player entering Legacy, I recommend Bant Miracles as a starting point. It plays Brainstorm, Ponder, Force of Will, Swords to Plowshares, and Terminus - all format staples you will use in any blue deck. The miracle mechanic with Terminus rewards skillful manipulation of your library. It also teaches you the most common matchups from the reactive side. Budget-wise, the expensive cards overlap with other blue decks so your investment transfers. Would you like a list to start with?

instruction: Should I play Reanimator or Sneak and Show as my first combo deck?
output: Reanimator is faster (can win turn 1) but more fragile to graveyard hate. Sneak and Show is slightly slower (turns 2-3) but more resilient because it does not rely on the graveyard. For a first combo deck, I recommend Sneak and Show because: it teaches you the blue cantrip shell, Force of Will protects your combo, and opponents need specific answers (Karakas, Containment Priest) rather than generic hate. Reanimator requires better knowledge of when to go for it versus wait. What is your experience with combo?

instruction: My local meta is all creature decks. What should I play?
