output: Against a creature-heavy local meta, you want decks with sweepers and efficient removal. Bant Miracles with Terminus and Swords to Plowshares dominates creature metas. Lands with Punishing Fire plus Grove of the Burnwillows and Tabernacle at Pendrell Vale grinds creature decks into dust. Mono-Red Prison with Blood Moon and Fury also excels. If you want to stay aggressive, Izzet Delver's Lightning Bolt plus Murktide Regent goes over the top of other creature decks. What is your preferred play style?

instruction: I want to play a tribal deck in Legacy. What are my options?
output: Legacy has several competitive tribal options! Goblins uses Aether Vial, Goblin Matron toolbox, and Muxus for explosive turns. Elves chains creatures together with Glimpse of Nature and wins with Craterhoof Behemoth. Merfolk uses lords for pump and islandwalk with Force of Will backup. Death and Taxes is loosely tribal (Humans) with Thalia and hatebears. Elves is the most competitive, Goblins is the most fun, and Merfolk is the most budget-friendly. Which creature type speaks to you?

instruction: How do I get better at playing around Force of Will?
output: Playing around Force of Will is a core Legacy skill. First, count their cards: Force requires pitching a blue card, so it is card disadvantage. If they are low on cards, they may not have it. Second, bait it: cast a less important spell first to draw out Force, then resolve your key spell. Third, sequence efficiently: in Storm, build storm count with rituals before the critical tutor. Fourth, use uncounterable effects: Cavern of Souls, Allosaurus Shepherd, Veil of Summer, and Defense Grid all blank Force. Which deck are you playing?

instruction: I enjoy midrange gameplay. What Legacy decks fit that style?
output: Legacy has excellent midrange options. 4-Color Control (Uro piles) plays value creatures, Uro, and countermagic for a grinding game plan. Bant Stoneblade combines Stoneforge Mystic equipment with countermagic and removal. Jund Midrange plays Tarmogoyf, Orcish Bowmasters, and hand disruption for a classic midrange feel. Cradle Control uses Green Sun's Zenith toolbox with Gaea's Cradle for explosive midrange turns. What colors do you prefer, and do you have any staples already?

instruction: I am interested in Dark Depths combo. How does it work?
output: Dark Depths combo uses two lands to create a 20/20 flying indestructible Marit Lage token. Dark Depths enters with 10 ice counters and creates Marit Lage when the counters are removed. Thespian's Stage copies Dark Depths, and the copy enters with zero counters, immediately creating Marit Lage. The combo requires only 2 lands and 3 mana total. You can play this in dedicated Lands (with Life from the Loam), Turbo Depths (with Crop Rotation and Sylvan Scrying), or Maverick (with Knight of the Reliquary tutoring). Would you like to know more about any version?

instruction: What Legacy deck is best for a competitive REL tournament?
output: For competitive REL, I recommend a deck that is both powerful and forgiving of small mistakes. Dimir Tempo is the meta king at 14%+ share with a proactive game plan that is hard to go wrong with. Sneak and Show is excellent because it often wins when it resolves a single spell regardless of game state. If you want a safer, grindy option, Jeskai Control has strong matchups across the board and rewards tight play. I would avoid high-variance decks like Dredge or extremely complex ones like Doomsday for your first big event. What is your experience level?

instruction: How do I know when to mulligan in Legacy?
output: Legacy mulligans follow different rules than other formats because the card quality is so high. Key principles: always keep a hand with a plan (cantrips alone are not a plan), ensure you can interact with the opponent (at least one Force of Will or removal spell against unknown opponents), and have mana that works (one land plus cantrip is often keepable in 18-land decks). Specific rules: keep any hand with turn-1 combo potential, mulligan hands with no interaction against unknown opponents, and be more aggressive mulliganing in games 2-3 when you know what to look for. What deck are you playing?

instruction: Is it worth investing in Legacy given Reserved List prices?
output: Legacy is worth investing in if you enjoy the format. Reserved List cards (dual lands, LED, City of Traitors) hold or increase in value over time since supply is fixed. Think of them as collectibles that also let you play Magic. Budget entry points exist: Oops All Spells, Burn, and Eldrazi need zero Reserved List cards. You can also start with shock lands and upgrade over time. MTGO is much cheaper if you want full-power lists immediately. What is your budget range?

instruction: I want a Legacy deck that rewards bluffing and mind games. What do you recommend?
output: For bluffing and mind games, Death and Taxes is the king. Aether Vial lets you bluff creatures, Rishadan Port creates complex mana decisions, and Flickerwisp enables surprise plays. Lands also rewards reads with its Maze of Ith timing, Crop Rotation bluffs, and Punishing Fire timing. Doomsday involves constructing piles based on what you think the opponent has. Painter lets you bluff by holding up Pyroblast mana. Which play style sounds most fun?

instruction: How do I transition from Modern to Legacy smoothly?
output: The transition is smoother than you think! Many Modern cards are Legacy staples: Murktide Regent, Orcish Bowmasters, Ragavan, and fetchlands all carry over. Start by identifying your Modern deck's Legacy equivalent: Modern Burn translates directly, Modern Izzet Murktide becomes Izzet Delver, and Modern Hammer has similarities to Legacy equipment strategies. The biggest differences are free spells (Force of Will, Daze), Brainstorm, and Wasteland. Learn these three cards and you are halfway there. What Modern deck do you play?

instruction: I want to play a white-based deck in Legacy. What are my options?
output: White has several strong Legacy identities! Death and Taxes is the premier white deck: hatebears, Stoneforge Mystic, and mana denial. White Initiative (White Stompy) uses Sol lands and Chrome Mox to power out fast creatures with Initiative. Stoneblade pairs Stoneforge with blue countermagic. Bant Control adds green for Uro and Endurance. Jeskai Control adds red for Lightning Bolt and Pyroblast. Mono-White Death and Taxes is the most purely white option. What appeals to you: aggro, midrange, or control?

instruction: What is the most beginner-friendly combo deck in Legacy?
output: Sneak and Show is the most beginner-friendly combo deck. The game plan is simple: cast Show and Tell or activate Sneak Attack, put Emrakul or Atraxa onto the battlefield, win. The blue cantrip shell (Brainstorm, Ponder, Force of Will) is standard across Legacy, so you learn transferable skills. Decision trees are cleaner than Storm or Doomsday. The main complexity is learning when to go for the combo versus wait. Reanimator is slightly harder but also straightforward. Would you like to know the key decision points?

instruction: I play Commander casually. Will I enjoy Legacy?
output: Many Commander players love Legacy! The card quality and power level are similar, but games are faster and more focused. If you enjoy powerful synergies and iconic cards, Legacy delivers that in concentrated form. Your Commander collection likely includes Legacy staples: Force of Will, Brainstorm, Swords to Plowshares, and Sol Ring-level power cards. The biggest adjustment is 1v1 dynamics and the faster clock. I recommend trying Legacy on MTGO first to see if you enjoy the pace. What kind of Commander decks do you play?

instruction: What Legacy deck should I play if I hate blue?
output: Great question - there are powerful non-blue options! Death and Taxes (mono-white) uses Thalia to punish blue players and Aether Vial to bypass countermagic. Eldrazi Stompy (colorless) slams Chalice on 1 to lock out blue cantrips. Mono-Red Prison deploys Blood Moon and Trinisphere to embarrass blue decks. Maverick (GW) attacks with creatures and land destruction. Lands (GR) controls entirely through lands. All of these have positive matchups against blue decks. Which colors do you prefer?

instruction: How do I prepare for a Legacy tournament if I have never played one?
output: Tournament preparation starts with knowing the top 10 decks: Dimir Tempo, Sneak and Show, Reanimator, Izzet Delver, Painter, Eldrazi, Death and Taxes, 8-Cast, Lands, and Storm. For each, know their key cards and how your deck interacts. Practice your sideboard plans for each matchup. Bring a written sideboard guide. Play at least 20 matches online to build muscle memory. During the event, eat and hydrate, take notes between rounds, and do not rush your plays. Legacy rewards patience and format knowledge. What deck will you be playing?

instruction: I enjoy playing lands-matter strategies. What Legacy options exist?
output: Lands is the ultimate lands-matter deck in Legacy! It uses Life from the Loam, Exploration, Wasteland recursion, Dark Depths combo, Maze of Ith, Punishing Fire, and Tabernacle to control the game entirely through lands. Turbo Depths is a faster version focused purely on the Dark Depths combo with Crop Rotation. Maverick uses Knight of the Reliquary as a land toolbox creature. Loam Pox combines Life from the Loam with Smallpox for land recursion. Which approach interests you more: grindy control or fast combo?

instruction: What Legacy deck has the highest ceiling for a skilled pilot?
output: Doomsday has the highest ceiling by far. A skilled Doomsday pilot constructs piles that beat specific combinations of interaction while requiring minimal resources. The deck has near-deterministic wins once you know the matchup and can identify what the opponent might have. Death and Taxes also has an extremely high ceiling because every game presents unique decision trees with Aether Vial, Flickerwisp, and Rishadan Port. Lands rewards format knowledge with its toolbox of utility lands. For pure skill expression, these three stand above the rest.

instruction: My friend plays Burn in Legacy. How do I beat it?
output: Against Burn, prioritize life gain and fast clocks. Swords to Plowshares gaining them life is actually fine because you need to kill their creatures. Uro Titan of Nature's Wrath is a nightmare for Burn: lifegain, card draw, and a recurring threat. Batterskull's lifelink stabilizes quickly. Chill (taxes red spells by 2) is devastating from sideboards. The best strategic approach is to race them: deploy efficient threats and make them spend burn on creatures instead of your face. What deck are you playing?

instruction: Is there a Legacy deck that plays like a puzzle?
output: Doomsday is the ultimate puzzle deck. Each game, you cast Doomsday and construct a 5-card pile from your library that must account for: your current mana, cards in hand, cards in graveyard, and what interaction the opponent might have. No two piles are the same. The EPIC Storm (TES) also plays like a puzzle during combo turns, calculating storm count, mana generation, and tutor sequencing. If you enjoy cerebral gameplay where every decision matters, these two decks are for you. Are you comfortable with complexity?

instruction: What is the cheapest competitive Legacy deck I can build in paper?
output: The cheapest competitive Legacy decks in paper are: Oops All Spells at approximately $800-1100 (zero dual lands, Tier 1 combo), Burn at approximately $1200-1500 (zero dual lands, zero Force of Will), and Manaless Dredge at approximately $500-800 (literally no mana-producing lands). All three can win tournaments. If you want a fair deck, Death and Taxes is around $2000-2500 with no dual lands needed. Eldrazi Stompy is similar at $2000-2500 with Sol lands being the priciest components. Which play style interests you?

instruction: I have a complete Dimir Tempo deck. What should I build next?
output: With Dimir Tempo, you already own Underground Seas, fetchlands, Brainstorm, Ponder, Force of Will, Murktide, and Bowmasters. Several decks share those cards! Doomsday adds only the combo shell (Doomsday, Dark Ritual, Thassa's Oracle). Sneak and Show shares the blue cantrip base and adds Show and Tell and big creatures. Reanimator shares the black disruption and adds the reanimation package. UB Shadow is nearly the same 75 with Death's Shadow instead of some threats. What play style do you want to explore?

instruction: How do I learn to play Brainstorm correctly?
output: Brainstorm is deceptively complex - it is often called the most skill-testing card in Magic. Key rules: never cast Brainstorm without a fetchland unless desperate. Put back cards you know you do not need, not cards you might need. Use Brainstorm in response to discard (hide two cards from Thoughtseize) and in response to shuffle effects. Do not Brainstorm just because you can - wait until you know what you are looking for. Practice with intention and track how often your Brainstorms led to good outcomes. Which deck are you learning?

instruction: I am torn between Elves and Goblins. Help me decide.
output: Both are tribal creature decks but play very differently. Elves is a combo deck that chains creatures into a massive Craterhoof Behemoth turn, winning from nowhere. Games end decisively. Goblins is a grindy toolbox deck that uses Goblin Matron to find answers and Muxus for explosive turns but also wins through incremental value. Elves is more competitive in the current meta but requires precise sequencing. Goblins is more flexible and fun with more interesting decisions each game. Do you prefer decisive combo turns or grindy attrition?

instruction: What Legacy deck has the fewest bad matchups?
output: Dimir Tempo has the most balanced matchup spread in Legacy, which is why it is the most played deck. It has game against everything: disruption for combo, threats against control, and tempo against midrange. No matchup is truly awful, though Eldrazi Stompy and Lands can be challenging. Jeskai Control also has a well-rounded matchup spread with strong removal and countermagic for any situation. If you want to never feel helpless, either of these is a great choice. What is your preferred play style?

instruction: How do I evaluate if a new set has Legacy-playable cards?
output: When evaluating new cards for Legacy, apply these filters: Does it cost 0-2 mana? (Legacy's most impactful cards are cheap.) Does it provide card advantage or free value? Is it better than what already exists in that slot? Does it have an ETB or cast trigger? (Creatures must provide value immediately before removal hits them.) Does it interact favorably with Brainstorm, fetchlands, or Force of Will? Cards that pass these tests are potential Legacy staples. Recent examples: Orcish Bowmasters and Psychic Frog both passed every filter.

instruction: I love the idea of cheating big creatures into play. What are my options?
output: Legacy excels at cheating creatures! Sneak and Show uses Show and Tell (put any permanent) and Sneak Attack (haste creatures for 1R). Reanimator uses Reanimate and Exhume to bring back discarded fatties. Natural Order in Elves sacrifices a green creature to find Craterhoof. Aluren lets you cast 3-mana creatures for free. Aether Vial puts creatures in for free at instant speed. Green Sun's Zenith finds and deploys green creatures. Which sounds most exciting to you?

instruction: What Legacy deck is best for grinding long tournaments?
output: For long tournaments, you want a deck that minimizes mental fatigue. Burn is excellent: decisions are straightforward, games are short (letting you rest between rounds), and the deck is consistent. Dimir Tempo is another good choice because the cantrip shell means you always have something to do and rarely face completely hopeless positions. Avoid complex combo decks like Doomsday or Storm for long events unless you are very practiced. Death and Taxes is deceptively tiring because every game requires unique decisions. What is your stamina like?

instruction: I want to play Legacy but only have Pioneer staples. Where do I start?
output: Some Pioneer staples cross over! If you have Monastery Swiftspear, Eidolon, and burn spells, Legacy Burn is close. If you have Thoughtseize, you are partway to Dimir Tempo or Reanimator. Fatal Push, Fable of the Mirror-Breaker, and various planeswalkers see Legacy play. The biggest gap is the blue cantrip shell (Brainstorm, Ponder, Force of Will) and the mana base (fetchlands, duals). Start with a deck that minimizes those gaps. What Pioneer deck do you play currently?

instruction: How should I approach Legacy if I have unlimited budget?
output: With unlimited budget, build Dimir Tempo first - it is the best deck and teaches Legacy fundamentals. Then build Sneak and Show and Reanimator since they share blue staples. Your Underground Seas, fetchlands, Force of Wills, and cantrips transfer across all these decks. For variety, add Death and Taxes (completely different play style, teaches the creature side of Legacy), and Lands (the most unique strategy in Magic). Five decks cover every major play style and most of your cards overlap. Where would you like to start?

instruction: What is the most fun Legacy deck regardless of competitive viability?
output: Fun is subjective, but Legacy has some truly unique experiences! Lands controls the game entirely through lands - no spells needed. Nic Fit ramps to absurd cards like Primeval Titan and Ugin using Veteran Explorer and Cabal Therapy. Cephalid Breakfast wins by targeting your own creature with a 0-mana ability a million times. Aluren has an infinite combo with a dungeon-delving zombie. Doomsday is a puzzle game disguised as Magic. Enchantress creates a pillow fort of enchantments. What kind of fun are you looking for?

instruction: I want to play Legacy Enchantress. Is it viable?
output: Enchantress is viable but niche, sitting below 1% meta share. Its strengths are: the Energy Field plus Rest in Peace lock prevents all damage, Argothian Enchantress and Sythis draw tons of cards, and Solitary Confinement makes you nearly untouchable. Weaknesses include vulnerability to Teferi, Time Raveler (shuts off instant-speed interaction), Council's Judgment (non-targeting removal through Solitary Confinement), and fast combo. It excels in creature-heavy local metas. Are you willing to accept some tough matchups for a unique experience?
